This machine is small and mighty. It produces a lot of ice and has a decent size storage bin. The scoop provided is kinda small and shallow so I bought a 38oz round scoop which is perfect. I have a small restaurant and so far it's keeping up with ice demand. I am using the -1 to 0 on the ice size and getting ice about every 15 minutes. The thicker the ice cube, the harder it is for the ice to break apart when the sheet of ice falls off the ice plate. It is fairly quietly, but when the ice drops, it's quite noisy and can be quite startling. I do keep getting an error message when the ice that comes out in a large sheet can't fall into the bin and blocks the sensor to make ice. Tried to message the seller re: the error message, but has not responded to my message and it's been over a week. (SMH) So, the machine is a good buy (even though a little pricey for its size) if you want a small machine that makes a lot of ice, but concerned about the error message since it happened within days of plugging in and producing ice.
So, it's been a few weeks and still getting the E2 error message. Still no return message from the seller. That's bad customer service. Also, a side note: the opening is kinda small because the door doesn't push in all the way. The door edge is sharp and I keep scraping my the top of my hand/ fingers when scooping ice.

I purchased the water filter to replace a Whirlpool Gold Model GS5SHASNL00 (part number 4396508).  Delivery was very prompt.  I bought the 2-pack; both were in excellent condition.  The filter installed with no problems and after 3 days of use, does not appear to be leaking anywhere.  The reason I did not give 5 stars is that the Whirlpool slide on “thumb & finger tab” must be removed from the old filter and slides onto the new filter.  The old filter had a design that latches the “finger tab” in place; this replacement filter did not have that.  I was concerned that the lack of a “lock-in” or “snap-in” design might be an issue.  So far it has worked.

One problem, it washes TO well with some items, even on gentle it has a very strong cycle/current and some delicate materials will be snagged if you don't put them inside of a washing bag or tied up pillowcase. There are some squared off edges inside the wash basin where the lint collector is, the delicate materials can get snagged and torn on it in the strong current.

The spin function is excellent, gets items almost dry within a minute it seems. It's NOT a "dryer" as some people have claimed, it's just the spin cycle.

The draining function works pretty well, but there will still be a thin layer of water in the bottom, it's not perfect. And definitely turn it off as soon as the water is mostly gone, leaving it on when there is no water to drain can ruin the pump.

I use a bucket to fill it up, as its faster, but the water tube would work just fine if I could figure out a way to attach it to the faucet.

I had a previous ice machine for about 4 years before it crapped out so I can at least say I'm not new to owning ice machines. I've owned this ice machine for 6 weeks now. In the time I've had it, I've had numerous issues with it:
- The ice machine sometimes doesn't make any ice at all
- It'll show that there is no water when it's full of water, and therefore, won't make any ice
- It shows that it's full when it's completely empty
- The on/off button doesn't work to turn the machine off.

When the machine works, it works well. I've had more issues with it than I should for a product that's fairly new. I've tried to turn the machine on and off with the on/off button when the machine seems to be stuck and not making ice, but that button only seems to turn the machine on. The only fix I've had so far is that I have to unplug the machine, plug it back in, and turn on the machine using the on/off button. I've had to do that several times a week now.

Do yourself a favor and get a different machine or at least purchase the extended warranty.

My 8 year old Sears Kenmore electric dryer (model# 110.62722101) started making squeaking noises when operating. Internet searches identified that the problem was likely due to a worn belt and old idler pulley and it was recommended that these items be replaced with the Whirlpool 4392065 Belt Repair Kit (it comes with the belt, idler pulley, two support rollers, four tri-rings and one metal washer that goes on the left support roller bracket). I found a video on You Tube as well as a better one on repairclinic.com which provided step by step instructions. The package also has instructions but the repairclinic.com video was much more helpful. Amazon offered the best price for the OEM recommended replacement item, and with my Amazon Prime membership, I got it in two days. The first package was missing the support rollers and Amazon rectified that by shipping out a replacement package in one day-nice touch. The parts were easy to replace but I recommemded a second person assist you in holding the dryer drum during the belt removal and reinstallation process as it makes lining up the drum on the back and front panel sections much easier. The most difficult part was removing the old tri-rings (I recommend using a very small flat head screwdriver and prying off the tri-rings at the opening near the inside tips of the triangles. By doing this job myself I saved at least $100-$200 dollars when compared to what Sears wanted to charge me to come and fix the problem.  My dryer runs like new again, no squeaking and the piece of mind that my dryer will last many more years. I highly recommend this product, but make sure you get the product made by Whirpool and not some generic dryer belt repair kit.

I thought the unit was defective and I called Amazon and they send me a replacement unit. To my surprise, it had the same problem. At this point I open the control module and to my surprise, the module was hardwired and all the speed control components were removed from the board and the fans were hardwired directly to the AC power. The three speed buttons were doing absolutely nothing. This is what I call a Chinese cost reduction, remove the speed components, save money, but leave the description as a three speed selector. It is either that, or the factory has no quality control and they are installing the wrong control module. I am returning both of them and I hope that Amazon stops selling this unit. It is a scam.

[[VIDEOID:bcdb4e7eeceeac7adcb93fffaa351712]] Just received these filters today and was excited to try one out. The replacement was very simple and the instructions were very good (a little small font size but very detailed).

You are instructed to run water through the new filter for 3 gallons or for about 5 minutes to condition the new filter. I believe this is pretty important as not only does it condition the new filter but also gets rid of any residue that may be in the filter.

When I first started the process of running water through the new filter, water and air shot out rather quickly but did not go all over the place or anything; just kind of startled me for a second. The first couple of glasses were somewhat cloudy so it was a good idea to run water through it as instructed.

Finally, since I am not a connoisseur of water, I took a nice full glass of clean shiny water to my wife (who is) and she said it tasted great. She did notice a difference with the new filter and was glad I installed it.

Got the dreaded E90 on my Frigidaire washer after 10 years. Most common advice is to replace the circuit board. The board & service would cost almost as much as a new machine - ugh. We're pretty handy so we figured we'd give it a try before calling in for service. Success, and still running fine after a month!
Pointer 1: Be sure of which product you need (use the model tool) and get the cheapest Amazon repackage - someone else's mistake is your savings :). Pointer 2: Find a youtube video that shows sufficient detail and keep going back to it. Pointer 3: Use a towel/bowl to keep from losing screws/buttons. Pointer 4: Take pictures as you disassemble - screws, wires, panel placement, everything! (you'll thank yourself later) Pointer 5: Be careful finding all the buttons & springs and transferring them. It's a bit tricky to get it perfect and close the box without them jumping around. Again, use that towel. Pointer 6: Ensure the dial is aligned correctly. Ours wasn't (180 off) and we needed to rotate inside the board with a flathead screwdriver to fix it. Plug it in and test that normal (middle) = 50 min and every position should display a time that makes sense; if it doesn't look quite right, do it again. Don't close up until you're sure of the alignment (don't be like us).

[[VIDEOID:7af3a5a27fd1619e5b76600e7f30974a]] I was really excited about how this washer machine performed. I got this because I always have some loads of rags, undergarments, etc. that I’d like to wash right away but don’t want to use the regular washer and waste the water on tiny loads. With this small machine I can process just what I need without much waste.  As for the unit, it’s very light when empty, I can carry it myself to wherever I want it to be. One issue I have is my bathroom faucet doesn’t fit with with the included water pipe, so I just use a pitcher to put water into the washer; might be unique to my faucet though. Also the washing process is a bit tedious since you have to release the water yourself and I suggest you make sure you cover the top with your thumb before you release it in the sink because the water comes out really fast. But it’s not too big a hassle for the overall portability.  As for operation, the torque power is pretty strong and turns garments easily in the machine and you can see clearly what’s going on inside with its clear shell. The timing controls let you easily set the length of operation and start/stop the process. After washing and draining, you fill up the water again to clean the detergent on the garments and drain again; I like to rinse twice to be sure no residue. To spin dry, you affix the basket in the machine, put items in and then let it spin. Personally I think the basket is bit small, but considering the part has to keep the machine stable, I guess it’s necessary. Garments come out damp but not soaking, and hang dry pretty quickly. Has not damaged any of my garments. The manual is really simple, and might could use a little more suggestions, I can offer the following from my experience. The included basket is for spin only not for anything else. And be sure to balance your garments when spinning, otherwise machine will vibrate and move; if balanced it is very stable. Overall I have really enjoyed this small machine and think it’s perfect for anyone needing to do small loads or needs a mobile washer.

I'll update this over the next few months, but after the first two weeks of using this Bosch my wife and I are nothing but pleased.

We have three boys who eat a lot and subsequently we run the dishwasher one or two times a day. The first time I ran this, I couldn't hear it at all and had to put my hand on it to verify it was running. Also opened it to make sure - water was dripping as typical when you open a washer mid-cycle. If it's quiet in the house, you can hear it running but barely. You can also hear when the washer is purging water before and after the rinse cycles through the drain but that's not bad either. But if you have the TV on or have kids in the house, you generally can't hear it at all.

But if it's quiet but doesn't clean, what's the point? We've had no issues with cleaning due to the washer. We have hard water in our area and the only issue we had some problems with spotting, etc. was when we didn't have the rinse agent in with standard detergent or using our usual special detergent. That was the same with any of the washers we or our friends have or have had.

Both the top and bottom baskets have lots of slots for things and at least 50% of them will fold down to accommodate whatever you are trying to wash.

Also, there's a timer to let you know how long the wash cycle has left. Not essential but nice.

Only issue we've had in two weeks is the door is extremely heavy (I believe a spring loose or missing). It has slipped or people new to using it have just let it drop. It goes fast enough I have worried about our dog. With the repeated drops the door has gone past horizontal. I scheduled a tech repair today and will update this once we see what normal should be.

But again, no issues with cleaning or sound. Both have been great for the first two weeks of use. You'd hope that'd be the case. Will update after we get the door squared away and again at the six-month mark.

--------------update on door-----------------
The door issue was as the comment from Lynn said - contractor screws that were too long. After the fix by Bosch (who's repairman said this was fairly common for appliances installed by contractors), the door is back to out-of-the-box condition.

New observations:
-the insulation on this thing is amazing. If the cycle is done running even two hours previous, the dishes and air inside is still really warm. I'm guessing once hot water goes in, it stays hot.

-drying dishes can be an issue if you run out of rinse agent or seem to run it on Auto Wash vs. Regular Wash. I'm going to keep a better eye on this as well.

-if you have any back issues, the bottom rack is extremely low to the floor. Likewise since the controls are on the door, there's more room in the top of the washer too. Again, lots of room for almost anything you could stick in it.

Again, overall satisfaction is high. We'll see where we're at in two more months.

I love this coffee maker. It has instant hot water. I have had several Keurig coffee makers, but their high price caused me to settle for a Chulux single serve k-cup coffee maker. The Chulux is a great alternative, but I missed the speed of brewing on a Keurig. You have to wait for the water to heat up before it will brew. I found this Cuisinart coffee maker that works exactly like the Keurig, but with a great price. Now the reason why I’m writing this review. To have instant brewing on the Keurig, you must set the timer to turn on for a window of time. If you brew a cup of coffee during that time period, the water is preheated. That way you get instant start because you don’t have to wait for the water to heat up. The Cuisinart has instant hot water. No waiting for the water to heat. To test this I unplugged the coffee maker, waited for 6 hours, then plugged it in and immediately brewed a cup of coffee. It took this machine less than two minutes to brew the coffee. I turn it on and off with every cup, because there is no difference in brew time. I believe that this will cause less scaling because I used to let the Keurig stay hot 3 hours per day.

If you are looking for a fully integrated solution for monitoring your home from your smartphone the GEENI Smartphone app will do it. Everything from Video to home lighting, to Pet treat machines, Pet watering, you name it. This Temp & Humidity is also fully integrated so you can monitor it all from your smartphone in one easy app.

Realize that with this full integration of everything on your phone the app is going to ask for a lot of permissions to access all the various features on your phone. I'm a bit stingy on that so I only allowed very limited functions for the Temp/Humidity sensor.

Also realize that the unit requests your wireless access code to pass to the device to access your wireless. I didn't see any harm to that as most of the kids in the neighborhood know the code and use it when they visit.

I did run into a problem setting up the device through their app on my phone. I don't know if it was me or I was a bit impatient but I went to lunch and when I came back it was all connected.

There is a steep Geeni app learning curve to fully integrate this and all the other Geeni devices into their single cohesive Geeni app on your phone. It's possible but just be prepared to spend some time with it.

I liked the fully integrated app devices approach that Geeni has taken. It certainly makes it easy if all the devices are rolled into one app than 10 different apps.

My only nick on this is the instruction manual. Two ++'s for a fully detailed step by step instructions manual with pictures but the size of type was a bit small for an older guy like me to read, even with glasses. I had to pull out my magnify glass to read through the details on the pages.

If you need a fully integrated system for your home Geeni should be one to consider.

I bought this Kenmore top load washing machine on June 30, 2022. It was delivered on July 6, 2022. We wash on avg 1 load a week. First of all, Amazon is selling Sears Kenmore's old products. Maybe Amazon didn't know, but Sears does. The defected item is the actuator. It keeps the washer in gear to do it's various cycles. Mine broke after only 4 months of light usage. Amazon washes their hands of anything they sell after a very short time. They do not help with a manufacture warranty other than to give you a phone number that is posted next to the product you bought online.  I have registered this product 10 times now with Kenmore. They don't seem to find that registration that I have done. That told me they were trying to avoid warranty work. The only way I could get them out was to set up a repair on my nickel. My guess is they are avoiding warranty work by not allowing you to register the product online. When the technician came out he asked me when I bought it and I showed him the receipt. He took care of the registration and took care of the repair for free as it should have been. It only took 3 weeks to get it repaired. My advice to you is "DO NOT BUY A EXPENSIVE ITEM ON AMAZON ESPECAILLY AN ITEM THAT HAS BEEN SOLD BY A LARGE COMPANY THAT IS SELLING THEIR ITEMS ON AMAZON". [[VIDEOID:c506936a4250cd04e3ed0a8fe7e7767b]] I've been using this for about 5 months now and it works great. Just connect it up to a sink faucet. I bought a $10 wheeled stand at Home Depot so that I can easily store it away. It's a bit confusing at first how to customize the cycles but the way I do it is:

1. Plug in
2. Load laundry and add detergent on top and close the lid
3. Press the power button
4. Press Program to go to Gentle mode
5. Press load size depending on how much I put in: 1 is the smallest load and three is the largest load. It loops around so keep pressing until you get the number you want.
6. Press the Wash button to pick how many minutes each wash will take. This loops around too. I usually pick 8 minutes.
7. Press the Rinse button to pick how many rinses to do. This loops around too. I usually just pick 1.
8. Then I press the play button to start. With the above settings, the whole wash takes about 24 minutes. If I forgot to put something in while it's going, I press the play button to pause it, open the lid, add the items, close the lid, and click play to continue.

I have no issue with the seller/supplier on this valve assembly, fair price, arrived early and is working. Note: The original factory tape seal on the carton was opened and re taped, so I suspect it was maybe returned at one time and reshipped to me. Regardless, it is working.
NOTE: In the assembly process you need to pry out three plastic tubes from the old valve assembly. During this process you will tear up the ends of the old tubes, could cause a leak when reinstalled. No problem for the two outlets, clear vinyl tubing used (just cut off an inch of the vinyl tubing and push in the tubing). Same as the original.
The change I noticed is in the water inlet. The manufacturer is using the connection and loose piece locking device as used for the soft vinyl tubing (the old unit had a solid locking device). "I hope it holds". The water inlet tube is a firm gray colored tube with a tapered end, so you cannot cut off the end, plus this tube is a custom length ( I suggest you purchase a new tube, if you are purchasing this valve). Anyway, The tubing was just slightly scared from when removed from the solid locking insert, so I wrapped the tapered end of the tubing with some Teflon tape and pressed in the tube, so far so good. But if it starts to leak will need to replace this tube.
Suggestion to the sell of the valve assembly "suggest end users to replace the inlet gray plastic tube, when replacing the valve assembly". Maybe a few bucks more, but could save time and money in the long run.

Pros:
It really gets the clothes clean
It spins your clothes so they dry very fast
It is easy to pick up and move (truly portable)
Stores in a small space.
I like how the spinner has a brake when you open the lid.
Very little detergent is required.
Cons:
While washing, it ties the clothes into a huge knot, so it helps to occasionally swish
the clothes with your hand to untangle them (during washing directional short pauses).
The hose is worthless, unless you have a truly compatible spigot set up to attach it to. I just portage
the water from a container, a bit of a workout.
You can't use this on your floor, it has to be in a tub or somewhere with a floor drain, and it is
best elevated above the drain so the washer does not sit in the drained water. (if you use your tub)
The spinner is a wonder, but half the time, if the clothes are not situated just right, it sounds and
vibrates like an earthquake. I mean, it can be deafenly loud. Half loads cause this. You have to fill
it up when spinning, and place the clothes in untangled.
If you hang your clothes to dry, you have to shake them out, to get rid of the lint.
I have issues with detergent causing skin irritation, so I rinse the clothes two to three times
so this is a lot of work if you portage the water.

All in all, if you are limited for space and can work through its disabilities, it is worth the money.

I have had my Honeywell humidifier for over 8 years! I use it almost every day (filling it twice, which is about 2 gallons of water total) from October through April. I live in Mass., and the colder it gets, the dryer my apartment is due to the heating system.

The biggest complaint people tend to have about these humidifiers is the need to replace the filters often (and the cost). It depends, I think, somewhat on the water quality. For me, I need to change filters about 1x per month. Having these generic filters helps a lot as they are much less expensive than the Honeywell (about 50% less!). (Note: you know it's time to change the filter because it gets pretty grungy and it takes a lot longer for the tank of water to deplete.

Helpful hint: Flip the filter every time you fill the humidifier and it will likely last longer!

So it was not a perfect fit in my 2003 NuTone. The posts that mount to the housing needed to be unscrewed and reversed (the heads actually fit a screw driver adapter on a drill, so it was fast). Then the posts were too short to actually go through the housing to affix the nuts. So I pushed one side through and put the nut on about a 1/4 turn. Then I used a hard surface and pushed on the housing so that the other post went through the hole slightly (of course this bend the housing between the two posts), then I was able to put the other nut on slightly. I went back and forth tightening the nuts until the posts were even with the back of the nuts. Had no issue with fan blade, or reinstalling into the ceiling.
All and all, it was a money saver and did not take long. Its just really not an exact match for the NuTone, and like everyone said, needs a little manipulation to get it to work.

I had an Emerson ice maker that I bought used that lasted me 6-7 years. I bought this ice machine November 2019.

I had problems immediately with the ice filling up too full then it would fault out. This I learned was due to the transparent lid and the light sensor inside being fooled by daylight. That was fine, I covered it and it kept working as normal.

A couple months later, the water pump started making a whining noise. At first I thought my water level was too low but even when full it would occasionally make the screaming noise which was especially annoying when going to bed. I tried to resolve the issue but basically ignored it until it stopped making the noise.

Today (August 2021), I was inspired to leave this review because I went to get some ice and found an empty basket. After cycling the power and inspecting it after a while it seems the chiller system isn't even working. The fan is just blowing cool air so it's not pulling any heat whatsoever.

In hindsight I should have returned the product during the initial problems because now I am past the warranty period.

I'll try to not be completely negative and give some positives. Kept away from sunlight, this machine did its job and continously pumped out the ice for me. Door reversal instructions are *not* included with the fridge, but I have created instructions at the bottom of this review.

Power consumed (watts): 50-75 when cooling

Beware: the manual states that it “was NOT designed to store perishable foods. Store only bottled or canned beverages.” However, since my intention is to use this as a milk fridge for an espresso machine (and because I could find no reasonably-priced similar alternative), I wanted to verify its performance. Here are the test results...

Cools to 31-38F at max (with different temperatures throughout the fridge interior); cooling begins at 45-46F.
When ambient air is 85F (as a stress test):
* Cools at ~0.8F per minute
* Compressor is 120-130F
* Sides are 96-99F
* Glass front is 66-69F

Reversing the door: The product information page indicates that the door hinge is reversible; however, the manual includes no instructions for reversing the door. Being able to reverse the door hinge is also critical for my use case. I have found that the door may be reversed by following these instructions (which are not included with the fridge, but which I determined by examining the fridge I received):
1) lift back edge of hinge cover on top, and slide towards the front.
2) unscrew the hinge from the door bottom, then top, then remove the door
3) unscrew the hinges from the body
4) remove the screw caps from the non-hinge side and move them to holes where the hinges were removed
5) screw the hinges onto the body on the desired side
6) screw the hinge to the door top, then the door bottom
7) slide the hinge cover on the top of the hinge, pulling up on the back edge while pushing the center of the cover down so that it slides securely into place.

My first load of laundry was a medium to large load, sweat pants, a t-shirt, a pair of socks, a queen bedsheet, a queen flat sheet, 3 pillowcases and 2 bath towels. I measured the cap from the liquid detergent bottle at about ½ full. I washed, then drained. Then I filled again to rinse, adding ½ cap full of Downey, then drained. I am wondering if using a Tide Pod would be too much???

Next I put just the queen bedsheet into the spin side, I did 5 minutes twice. It came out just about dry, a little damp. I put it on my mattress and it was dry by the time I had repeated this with my queen flat sheet. I left my blanket off for about an hour to make sure that both the bedsheet and flat sheet were completely dry. Next were my 3 pillow cases, two were the same thin cotton material so I put them on the pillows. One pillow case was a little thicker cotton so I hung it overnight. I put in one bath towel at a time, I did have to hang them overnight to dry. I was disappointed that I did not get that soft, fluffy towel that you get from a dryer that I like though. I then put in the sweat pants, the t-shirt and pair of socks, they came out damp so I laid them out overnight to thoroughly dry.

My second load was small, two new pairs of black jeggings and a microfiber hand towel. I put everything together on the spin side for 5 minutes. I hung them to dry overnight as they were damp.

TWO WEEKS LATER 9/2/2020 – I am now more experienced with this machine. I washed 2 bath towels and 2 pairs of jeggings. I filled the water up right above the items with ½ cap of detergent. Drained. Filled again to rinse. Drained. Filled again adding ½ cap of Downey. Drained. I switched the water to the spin side and turned it on. Yes, it will drain right out. With the water running, I put each item into the spin side one by one, which slowed the drain. I put in the safety cover and pressed it down firmly on top of the clothes then turned it on for a 5 minute spin, the machine will be a little noisy with the water on. After about 1 minute, I turned the water off. The machine got quieter and kept spinning for the remainder of about 4 minutes more. Everything was a bit damp.

Keep in mind that this is a washing machine, this is NOT a dryer. Your items will NOT come out dry. Damp, but not dry. I did miss my soft, fluffy towels and wanted my jeans to shrink back into shape so I ended up purchasing a small, portable dryer to go along with this unit. My clothes were dry in about ½ hour.

CONS – Being in my shower, the hose just reaches my showerhead pipe exactly, I would like it a little bit longer to give it some slack. The original clamp it came with was too flimsy and would not hold. I ended up using a small hose clamp to attach it to my showerhead pipe. The power cord is only about 55” so I have to use an extension cord to reach the outlet. If the machine clunks loudly for about 2 to 3 seconds when it starts spinning, you need to balance the clothes and/or check that the safety cover is pressed firmly on the items. Unlike an automatic machine, you have to manually fill, drain and spin which means spending more time at the washing machine, not just leaving it as you would with an automatic machine. On the other hand, it is very convenient for my small condo unit where I don’t have a lot of space.

Overall I am VERY happy with this machine! It is very quiet while running except for when I start the spin with the water running, but then gets quiet when you shut the water off. I do like that I can use both sides at the same time. I am elated that I do not have to use our community washer & dryers anymore. I very highly recommend this machine. I did purchase the SquareTrade 5-Year Warranty to go along with it, just in case.

Before installing, I tested it on the bench (I'm an EE, so know how to do that safely). Powered up, the igniter drew 3.6 amps when fully hot, dissipating 424 watts as measured with a Kill-A-Watt electrical meter. Information I found online indicates that 3.2 amps should be a lower limit, so I knew that this one was good. Resistance measurements are not as useful, because it varies a lot with temperature - this one measured 94.1 ohms at 75 degrees, but 3.6 amps at 118 VAC indicates 32.7 ohms at full power.

The failed unit I replaced worked for about 2 years, but would no longer get the oven hot enough in time to avoid the F9 error code even though you could see it glowing. Removed, it drew only 2.4 amps (283 watts) when tested the same way.

The igniter itself is easy to install (2 screws and an electrical plug) once you get to it. Getting to it in my LG range is harder - you have to take the oven door off just to remove the oven floor.

My husband and I just moved into a beautiful new apartment. Only problem is there is no washer/dryer hook-up. The laundry facilities are nice, but I can’t get myself to roll my dirty laundry through my complex, grab quarters from the Bank, lug my detergent, all while running back and forth to move my clothes into the dryer, and wait to repeat the process for another load. After doing my research I decided to get the Della Mini after watching videos, ciphering through reviews, and comparing prices etc.
Pros:
Price $120 bucks! Worth it.
Size (comparable to a large hamper/fits perfect in the master bathroom)
Quality: it gets the job done! Clothes are clean in minutes.
Spin dry: clothes come out practically dry. I rarely need to put it on longer than 2 minutes. Now the cons...
Cons:
- the hose is short and does not fit American nozzles. Do yourself a favor and just fill a bucket of warm/cold water. It’s faster.
- the cord is short. Depending on your set-up you may need an extension cord.
- Spin dry: do NOT overstuff! It will shake and go off balance. You will have to take longer drying clothes.
Drain: hose may need to be lowered to have drain function kick in. Or, I’ll just lift the machine (it’s light) up a bit to activate the drain.
Care: dry out the tub and remove all moisture. My tub arrived with a yellowish mold inside. Although I paid for a new one! At first I was furious, but after seeing that it functioned fine I decided to avoid the hassle of returning. Be sure to dry out excess water with a towel or rag.
Overall this thing has been a lifesaver! I will update this post in several months. Good luck 😊

Ordered as replacement for failed ignitor on 15 year old GE gas stove. Arrived in timely fashion, well-packaged. Simple, straightforward installation. Upon first trial, as the element heated up towards max brightness, but before gas initiation, the element snapped in two, ending continuity, and of course, function.

Requested refund/replacement, and received the same about a week later. Installed as before. Trial passed; put oven back together, and baked a loaf of bread. Success! But, not so fast...the next time I went to preheat the oven...nothing. Took the oven apart again, removed the ignitor, and checked for continuity: there was none. Close visual inspection revealed what appears to be a break in the ceramic coil, just visible through the lowermost aperture in the attached photo, near the base of the ignitor.
Will be returning for refund, but no replacement.

I didn't know solenoid coils could wear out. I mean, there's no moving parts -- or rather, the coil moves other parts, but the coil itself does not move. It's just a spool of wire.

And yet, we found our clothes dryer not drying. After extensive research, the conclusion was the most likely culprit was the solenoid coils needed to be replaced.

Interesting Discovery: During my research, I discovered that all gas-fired clothes dryers appear to have the same heating innards. This makes some sense when you think about it -- once you have a design that reliably avoids burning people's houses down, there's no need for every manufacturer to make additional one-off tweaks.

I bring this up because, since all the heating innards are essentially identical, it's almost impossible to buy the wrong solenoid coils for gas-fired dryers. These fit perfectly into our cheap-o Admiral dryer, were easy to replace, and the dryer worked perfectly when the machine was put back together. Recommended.

The picture above shows the manual that has QR codes to download an app to run the program. I tried both QR codes to download the APPs. They asked me to set an email address and a password. Fine, but THEN, it asked for a CREDIT CARD NUMBER! While the screen told me I would not be charged anything, I decided not to take their word for it and backed out of the program.

I almost wrote a bad review at that point, BUT, I decided to go look for the "Smart Life" App in the Google Play store and see if it was different. YES! From the Google Play Store (I do not know if this works well from the iPhone App Store), it asked for an email address and password to set up an account, but then instead of asking for a credit card number, it simply had me verify my email with a 6 digit code they sent to it. NO CREDIT CARD NUMBER required when the app is downloaded from the Google Play Store.

Be sure to take note that this is a 2.4ghz device, so your wifi router must not be set to 5ghz. My Smart Life app originally tried to set up on the 5ghz wifi, as I think that app supports both...but this thermometer needed the 2.4ghz channel instead. Once I changed it, it worked fine.

So I am happy with the result, as I have it in a detached garage and have its alarm set to tell me if the temperature drops below 45 degrees. I can open the app and see exactly what the temperature/humidity has been.

I have never had an upright freezer (and I've had many over the years) that went out in less than 5 years.  On this one, both the condenser and the evaporator were dead and needed to be replaced.  I was told, over the phone, that my warranty was good for 5 years.  The repair company (who they recommended) called them and got the okay to come out to see what was wrong.  When they called in for the fix, they were told that the Frigidaire freezer was not under warranty.  After many phone calls, I found out that the warranty doesn't stay with the freezer. Since it was in the home that I purchased, the warranty was no longer any good.  The first person I spoke with had made a mistake.  So I ended up having to pay a $70 service call fee and now I have to worry about how to get rid of this worthless freezer.  I will never buy any product made by Frigidaire again, and I would strongly advise anyone thinking about it, to choose a product that (1) will last and (2) will honor its warranties.

I bought this washer after reading the many positive reviews at multiple sites . I am replacing a ten year old popular brand front loading washer that many of us have come to expect tens of years from. As you can see I will not be buying that brand anymore. The washer has plenty of features but the one I was most interested in is the perfect spin which works as advertised. This is what killed my old washer. This washer is hands down a better washer. It can complete a wash in as little as eighteen minutes and my clothes do not have a musty smell to them, my old washer always left a musty smell. I also have noticed that there is near zero lint in my dryer lint trap. I have owned this washer for just under three months. With that said, my expectations are high. I would  recommend this washer to anybody.

Nicely designed hood but they need to work on quality control. The sheetmetal plenum that the fan sits in was spotwelded slightly off center so the fan rubbed and made a racket when I first installed it. Being an engineer I am inclined to fix things myself and was able to modify the motor bracket to shift the fan over a bit to clear. I know 99% of people would not have done this but hopefully I just got the 1 hood that slipped through QC (if they did any). The sad part about this is that this hood is actually made in the USA...I would have expected this sort of quality problem from an offshore manufacturer! Would have probably given 5 stars if the housing had not been welded improperly as it is otherwise a good hood.

One the hood was fixed it does run well, is pretty quiet (some whine from the motor at low speed) and produces a hurricane at high speed. Wish the front vent for ductless operation angled the airflow up a bit so it didn't blow in your face when you are standing in front of the range. I do like the continuously variable speed control vs. most hi/lo/off controls.

The black wires (line) could have been another 2 or so longer for slightly easier wiring.

I have had no issues with these water filters. They have performed as expected. I’ve read some reviews that mentioned these filters leaked. I suspend those reviewers either received counterfeit filters, or they didn’t install them correctly. It’s inconvenient, but Samsung does instruct you to shut the water supply off to the fridge before replacing the filter, and failure to do so can result in leaking.

You may be tempted to go with a cheaper 3rd party water filter, but I suggest you compare the NSF certifications the 3rd party filters have. Most, if not all, have lower certifications than what the genuine Samsung filters offer. I didn’t find any, for instance, that were certified to filter out pharmaceuticals in the water supply (NSF 401). You may save money on a third party filter, but you’re getting an inferior product.

Our 15-year-old dryer has been squeaking and squealing for a long time. Then the heater went out. I was replacing the heater and figured while I'm inside I'd replace all of these parts, too. This kit makes for a cheap fix if you need these parts, or a cheap investment you're just looking to extend the life of your dryer (and your sanity, when it squeals). The pulley was probably the main culprit in my setup, but I think replacing all the parts now will reduce noise and potentially save me hassle down the road. The dryer is SO much quieter now.

The parts seemed comparable to the OEM parts. My only negative is that the metal axle of the rollers in this kit were not very smooth. I opted to keep the original axles installed, clean them and give them a little (non-flammable) lube, then stick the new rollers on them.

Also, note that at least in my dryer, the OEM idler pulley had a small flange where the screw goes through and connects near the motor. The one in this kit does not. At first, I was fiddling with washers to keep it from wobbling. Then I realized the screw provided in this kit is slightly shorter than the OEM one. It fits perfectly if you use the screw that comes with the kit and toss the old one.
Good value.

Ok keep in mind this review is being written before I have installed the hood and is about first impressions. The hood is nice, seems well made although somewhat lighter construction than I would have thought. All the bits and pieces are provided and the install manual looks to be very well done. I would have liked it to had Led lights instead of what it came with but it does look as if these can be changed out. One bit of design problem is that the hanging eyes are not offset so you can put up a board to connect to on the back .. and they are not spaced apart far enough to connect to 16 inch centers .. this results in some major work to get a stable hanging platform which will not pull loose and fall on the stove.
The packing around the outside of the unit was very good and no doubt saved it from being a total disaster. To say the shipper had dropped it more that once was putting mildly. Perhaps Z line should switch shippers. The parts which were enclosed inside the hood had come loose and were banging around which resulted in a small dent in the outer shell and another in the rack mount inside. Now normally these would not be of too much concern but when you pay full price for something,you expect it to not be damaged. On another note, why you might ask did I not say something to the UPS guy when it ws delivered ... well that was because he dropped it off across the highway at another house .. both houses have the same 1036 address but on different roads with completely different spelling. You cannot get them mixed up if you only read the darn label and the road signs. So i was not able to reject the shipment because of obvious exterior damages. What to do now ... I need to put up the hood so I can continue on with my kitchen remodel .. but the small dents will drive my wife nuts ..and I still don't know if it suffered any more damage inside .Well now I do know .. the impact on the outside caused things on the inside to bounce around causing more damage than i first saw ... when you pay over 1 thousand dollars for an item, you really expect it to be in good shape on when you get said item. After mulling it over for a few days we decided to contact UPS to see what could be done as they were the ones we thought could fix the problem .. no so .. you see as we had not shipped thru them, Amazon had, it was up to Amazon to make the claim. So on to Amazon ... not at first real good, you see they do not make it easy to ring them up and let them know something happened ... however Siri on an apple phone knew the phone number for customer service .. once connected things got better but it was still a pain in the butt .. we had to ship it back. once they receive it 7 days later we will get a full refund, that's good, not great but good, however what happens if for some reason UPS screws up again and ships it to the wrong place ..that's bad ... oh and they will send us another one should we decide we want one ...that's good but who is to say they won't ship it via UPS again who may well deliver it to the wrong address .. that's bad .. now again, its not so much that I have a bad impression of the hood or the company who made it .. its the shipping I don't think a lot of .. and the idea that its so hard to get to someone to talk about the problem .. part of the problem it seems is my fault .. in trying to be fair to the product, I gave it a 4 star before I was fully aware of the damage .. this did not trigger and look see by Amazon as to why there was a low rating on this product .. so now I have dropped the rating down to a 2 star again because of the shipping ... hope this helps ..

I was hesitant to get a square ice cube maker based on some reviews. Some claimed that an ice pick was required or that once you put the cubes in the freezer, they froze into a hard block. Yes - when the cubes fall into the ice bin, they often stay in a long rectangle. Yes - when I put them into the freezer to harden, they do stick together. I have the same problem when I buy ice from the convenience store. How do I fix that - I lightly tap the ice against a hard surface. I bought plastic ice bags to store up and save the ice I'm making from this machine. When I dump the cubes from the ice bin into the plastic bag, i lightly tap them on my kitchen counter. They're still wet on the outside when I stick them into the freezer. Once they're frozen hard, I give the bag another light tap on my kitchen counter. Perfect ice, no ice pick or heavy manual labor required.

This fit my Magic Chef dryer (model # MCSDRY1S) perfectly. It was easy to install once I figured out how to do it. The key is to remove the top 3 screws and the upper middle ones from the bracket 1st, and just loosen the lower middle ones (don't take them out yet!) Then carefully pry the top part of the bracket out just enough to slip part of the belt through, leaving the bottom half of the belt still on the outside of the bracket. Carefully slide the part of the belt you slipped through the top of the bracket down to the fan pulley. Then stop and put the top 3 screws back in tight, and the upper middle screws back in loose. Now remove the bottom 3 screws (and any zip ties attached down there) and gently pry the bottom part of the bracket out just enough to slip the remaining part of the belt through. Then slip belt over motor pulley.

The upper and lower middle screws cause the drum to shift when loosened so be careful. That's why I didn't take them out all at once.

I love induction, but this product has engineering problems.

I bought this because I wanted a motion sensor that could detect light levels and turn the lights on and off accordingly. I already had a motion sensor that worked well but would turn the lights on even in bright daylight.

The motion detection on the Aeotec is horrible. The range is very small and sometimes doesn’t even detect motion. I ended up using both sensors together to control the lights. With them working side by side, you really can see the difference in motion detection between the two.

The Aerotec does seem to detect light levels. I do not understand how it works as the lux reading does not change after the lights are turned on, but it seems to work the way I want it to. If the lux reading is below 40 and my motion detector senses motion and it’s between 5 am and midnight, it turns the lights on. If it’s between midnight and 5 am, only a couple of lights are turned on and only at 10%. You don’t want to blind yourself in the middle of the night getting water.

Overall I’m happy with it as it does what I wanted it to. It’s not the 6 in 1 sensor that they claim it to be. Automated lighting has become like electricity in our house. No one notices it until it doesn’t work.

When my wife purchased the Silonn Ice Maker I was skeptical and figured I would soon be making a trip to the UPS Store to return it to Amazon. When it arrived she cleaned it as recommended, added water, and turned it on. "How long is that going to take" I scoffed. To my surprize, plop, plop, it was making ice in just minutes.

The unit makes large and small cubes, though neither is particularly large. We (notice I'm using the "we" word now) make large cubes and they are perfect for drinks. We use the ice maker about twice a week as there is only the two of us; if you have a large family you may wish to consider something with greater capacity because it makes ice in relatively small batches.

The biggest drawback to the unit is that it doesn't keep the cubes that it makes frozen. When you make cubes you need to remove them from the unit and put them in the freezer ASAP, or they will partially melt and ultimately stick together in one big glob. We overcome this problem by putting the cubes in a heavy duty reusable zip-lock bag, and after the cubes have been in the freezer for awhile, whack the bag on the countertop.

For low volume users the Silonn Ice Make will work just fine. For us, it's WAY better than the ice maker that came with our Samsung refrigerator!

I've had this machine since 2006, and I am in love with it. I live in a rental in NYC in a building without any laundry room. This means that prior to this machine, my husband and I had to gather our clothing, and walk 4 blocks to a public laundry mat. Not only is that inconvenient, but you're sharing machines with absolute strangers is not that sanitary and this really hit home when we were doing laundry and a homeless man came to wash his clothing in the machine next to us.

First thing I have to say is, this machine is NOT for everyone. If I had the option of having a regular side by side washer and dryer, I would not have purchased/liked this machine. The machine is small enough to fit anywhere. I have it set up between the sink and refrigerator. Since it is small enough to fit just about anywhere, this means that your loads are going to be smaller as well. Personally I don't have a problem with load size....instead of doing laundry once a week, I do laundry every other day or every two days during the week.

I think the first thing people need to understand about this machine is that it's a trial and error process. When you first start using it you'll have problems getting a load done as sometimes the machine is "unbalanced" and cannot spin. When that happened to me I used to stop the machine, and reload it to make sure that it was balanced (making sure to have some heavy items in the bottom, smaller items in between, a couple of more heavy items on the top). A normal cycle takes 58 minutes....my clothing comes out super clean. Sometimes when I am in a hurry I'll do the "speed wash" cycle, which only takes 30 minutes.

Sure laundry takes a little longer because it's an all in one unit, but I have NEVER had a load longer than 3 hours. This weekend I washed a complete queen size sheet set (fitted sheet, 2 pillow cases, and a sheet) and it took 2:30 hours from start to finish. Did it come out wrinkled??? Yes, but I personally don't care since it's my bedding. Was it dried when I pulled it out....YES!!! That I do care about. I read some of these reviews and I am amazed at the reasons people give it low rating (can't wash another load while drying...really? you didn't figure that one out before you brought it?) Is it a perfect machine...no, but for people without regular machine options I think it great.

Size of the loads....this is something else you have to play around with. When I first started using the machine, I had read that you should be careful with your load size, so I was making my loads really small. I just finished a load right now which included 8 shirts, 2 shorts, 1 long pajama pants, 3 underwear....the total time for this load, 2 hours (using speed wash, 30 minutes, and drying with timer set at 1:30 hour). My load was clean and dry. Taking your clothing out of the dryer is something else you have to get used to because the load will feel damp, but it's not. All you have to do is shake the condensation out (and I really do mean shake. What I do is when I pull it out of the machine, I shake it and set that piece of clothing on the back of the dining room chair. By the time I am done pulling everything out, all the clothing is dry and ready to be folded and put away.

Vibrations/Shaking...the machine is fairly quiet. The only thing that make a loud noise is when it's expelling water and even that is tolerable. I can watch tv in the other room and it doesn't bother me. When the machine spins, it spins very fast (which helps with making sure your clothing is almost dry by the time the wash is done) but this means that there will be some vibration. My floor is wood, and you don't really hear it. Now if I touch the counter or stove I can feel the vibration....but not hear it. My downstairs neighbor had never complained.

I've also read people complain about a musty smell inside the machine and I've never had an issue like that. I also make sure to take care of my machine. After I am done using it I let it cool off a bit (the time it take for me to put the clothing away). I take a piece of napkin and make sure to dry out the water that stays between the drum and rubber. After I am done with that, I do not close the machine but leave it open for a couple of hours. I also remove the detergent dispenser, rinse it, and leave it open as well so it dries out.

I have to admit that I have never cleaned out the inside of the machine for lint...but I plan to have my husband do it soon. I printed the instructions that I got from someone who reviewed the machine here. But even with all this time using it and not cleaning it properly, I really haven't had an issue. I bet when I clean it out my drying time is going to take even less.

In conclusion, if you live in an apartment where regular laundry hook-up is not available and are tired of going to public laundry mats or sharing machines, you should really consider buying this washer/dryer unit. But do so with the idea that it does not work the same as a regular dryer and therefore you need to adjust the way you do laundry.....but the advantages of having your own machine in the apartment far outweighs any negatives this machine may have. I wish I could hug whoever invented this machine :)

I read some of these reviews and all I can say is WAAAAH. So you have to stick a pen in the bottom to pop the filter out. That's the only thing those "compatible" filters are good for. Second, I use a milk bag to filter the grind. Most of the pre-ground coffees are full of grind dust making mud when you cold brew it. Hence the multiple filters. Some of you may not be as critical of their home brew as i am. I've been cold brewing years before it became popular and used to grind my own. If you grind I suggest a good burr grinder and absolutely not a blade grinder. Blade grinders produce quite a bit of dust which, as I said, turns to mud and clogs your filters making them junk, having to be changed long before they should be. Just to be clear, this is how I cold brew. I'm not telling anyone else how to do it, just making suggestions that some might find useful.

Great so far! We've made 12 shots of espresso using these little cups and they generally work really well. I would give them 5 stars except for a couple issues:

Our house was built late 90s with GE profile appliances. Being conscious about environment, we follow a rule of not replacing an appliance until it is broken. Recently, our dishwasher went broken with very bad noise coming out of motor. The problem is we have an opening of 33" and the dishwasher is about 3/8" dropped below the floor. So, even though there is enough height inside, it is impossible to put most dishwashers (over 33") in without opening the counter. We looked at all the models of GE, Maytag, LG, Samsung, etc etc and couldn't find anything that has adjustable an height. Eventually, a sales person at Sears quietly told us we should look at Miele in another store. This turns out to be a great idea. Miele European model can adjust from 32" to 34" with almost same capacity like their US model. It is a lot more expensive but we figure one of these days we need to remodel our kitchen and we want to buy something that we can keep in the future kitchen. It is very nice that we didn't have to wait for long time to get an EU model. The installer came in and was able to cut the old GE out and installed a new Miele. Here is what we have seen:

1. Miele allows installer to raise the front two supports easily and the entire machine is raised at the same time. This let the installer make a perfect fit to our space. Smart engineering!
2. Their front panel is hard to put on without a professional help because it comes in as a separate piece so that customers can customize it to whatever they like. Great designs but there are many small things you have to take care properly. Our installer is certified Miele dealer. They had no issues.

We have used the dishwasher for a few weeks.

1. Absolutely easy to use and dishes come out very very clean. Grease and dry good come out great. It definitely has more washing power than our old GE. No comparison.
2. Very quiet. The only sound coming out is when it flushing water. Even that is a lot less noisy than our old GE. It doesn't seem like it flushes as much as GE. The motor sound is very low and very pleasing to hear (if you try hard to actually hear it).
3. It doesn't have a heat coil like GE did. We like it a lot. It uses less power and it doesn't burn plastics if they are at lower part of the DW. Also, less risk for getting burned. Dishes come out 95% dry. if you simply leave everything there for another hour or something, it becomes almost 100% dry. We like this. We usually start the DW before going to bed. In the morning, everything is dry anyway.
4. It doesn't use the overflow thing that our old GE used. For my old GE, there were a few times that water came out of the overflow to our wood floor when drainage was slow in the kitchen. Miele doesn't use this. It detects when slow drain is detected and just stop. This is smart! We noticed may new appliances still use overflow.

Because we used the certified Miele installer from the dealer, they gave us one extra year warranty plus the original one year.

Very expensive (2-3 times more $ than we originally wanted to spend) but we are very happy to have it. In fact, we may go with other Miele appliances since their "simple elegant style" is a better fit for us than a more aggressive "I am expensive" Viking type of appliances.

After reading the reviews, I decided to try these, since I wasn't too thrilled with either the cost or quality of the senseo pods. After getting my three sets in the mail, I went to my grocery store, bought the Starbucks Sumatra whole beans, ground them to expresso grind, and went to try the ecopads. I followed the instructions, put the ecopad in the machine (perfect fit), hit the 8oz button, and wa - la! I would challenge anyone to tell the difference between the stuff you pay $3.50 a cup for at Starbucks. I'm definately hooked. I can't see how other reviewers have had problems with these. They are easy to fill, and fit perfectly in the machine. They are a bit messy to clean up, but its a fair trade off for the awesome coffee and the cost savings over the pods or actually going to starbucks. I would estimate I will get 50-60 cups out of a $6 store bought bag of coffee. Basically, one heaping teaspoon fills up the ecopad perfectly. Also I would estimate getting hundreds of uses out of each ecopad - they seem very durable. I am looking forward to trying different coffee's with these. Well worth the tiny investment!

It doesn't actually clean the dishes, no matter what setting we have it on. It leaves bits of food and random stuff on everything. It smells like rotten eggs when it runs. The good: the tub itself is well made. The bad: the support rails are not well made. The ugly: those rails again. Cheap: the rails, the suction cups, and the cost which is about four dozen dollars currently. The "undirty": fifty cranks of a pair of jeans and a light top with about
a gallon of water and a tiny bit of liquid detergent came very clean. So, for some who need a low water, small space solution this is going to be a real win. I wanted an air vent at the bottom of my shed to allow air into it, but keep critters out. So, when this product became available, I ordered it. Here is what I found:

1. Intended to go on a 6 inche vent, it is designed to fit into a 6 inch hole and has two screw points to mount it and three tabs to hold a 6 inche bent pipe.

So, it was easy to install into my shed, just cut the hole and put on a ring of silicon around it, then insert screws.

2. The unit has louvers and is shaped such that it is protected from most rain, just not 90 degree sideways wind blown. The louvers keep the rain out for sideways wind.

3. There is a screen guard on it as well as the lovers to keep critters out and bees out.

This does what I wanted. When my temperature controlled fan come on to ven the shed, I now have good airflow into the shed from a low point which vents the heat.

I ended up purchasing some aluminum tape, specifically designed for heated duct work, and wrapped all of the seams on the vent elbow, as well as where it connected to the longer hose. I wanted so badly to love this product. And for about the first year of owning it we did. After about a year, the icemaker started to act up. I had made sure to clean it every month and de-scale every quarter. Nonetheless, it began to become less efficient and make less and less ice. After several cycles of de-scaling, defrosting, taking it apart and cleaning the interior, I finally decided to reach out to GE and see what my options were. They told me the warranty had just expired and that I could pay them a couple hundred dollars to get it repaired, so I sent it in. After receiving it back I swear nothing was different. The picture I’m posting here is after 24 hours of receiving it back from GE and turning it on. We never used any ice and the bin never got above halfway full. Eventually the light started blinking yellow again like it did before I sent it in.

So I guess it is an awesome icemaker if you only intend to have it for about a year. At that point the warranty will expire and it will start acting up on you. I had three other friends purchase the icemaker within a six months based on my initial recommendation, and all but one experienced the exact same thing. And unfortunately GE will not stand behind their products for more than a year. if you do buy it, make sure to get the extended warranty. Though I don’t know it is worth the hassle of having to send it in and deal with it. ￼

I've had this G.E. profile for slightly less than 4 years.  It was OK at first.  But in the first year two of the drawers have broken, the ice makers leaks down the refrigerator and onto the floor, the temperature control does not control the temperature on either side and the freezer thaws intermittently and my TV dinners get soggy, and yesterday both the refrigerator and the freezer gave out and stopped cooling. It still makes an excellent nightlight though. Oh and the ice maker gets clogged and then won't make ice.  A little research on the net and I have discovered the motherboard breaks on these puppies and they stop cooling.  I mean I have literally found DOZENS of unhappy owners who have had their machines no more than 4 years.  Some less than one year and the cooling system stops.  Figure a $700 bill for that, and figure the new mother board will last you about 1-4 years before it breaks too. This is a pervasive problem. If you figure one in every 10 people who have this same problem with their G.E. Profile's, write a complaint on line, then it's pretty much... yeah, everybody whose had one for 4 years. You'll be lucky to get 2 or 3 years from this machine.  (by the way, G.E. dishwashers suck too.  I know, I got a house full of new high end G.E. appliances furnished by the builder and I've had trouble with the dishwasher (recalled) the dryer and especially this dog of a refrigerator.  A word of advice:  When looking on line for reviews, do not trust the # of stars, you have to read the actual reviews because most of the people writing the reviews do so within a month or two of getting their appliance--sometimes the day it arrives. What the heck use is that, I ask you? (this is true of all appliances) What can they tell you on day one?  It arrived fast?  It's pretty?  Yeah that's gonna matter in a year when your shrimp and steaks are all thawed and the freezer is leaking all over the floor and the dog is licking the puddle. Try to read reviews from people who have had the machine for YEARS and that will tell you something.

11/18/2021 Update
Thanks to all for all the thumbs up received on this review. I was just rehydrating my humidor today & noticed there's a 5% difference between its analog hygrometer vs this digital one. Analog read 5% higher. For the price, this digital one is worth every penny. I hate soggy & dry smokes.

I like how well this product works so far. The only reason I didn't give it five stars is not because of the products inability to work, but rather its design could be enhanced a bit more. Specifically, instead of the adhering stickers being on the bottom of the product, the option to adhere them in areas on top would've been nice. In this manner, I could adhere it to the surface of my humidors window and see the humidity readings without needing to open up the humidor box each time. Again, not a big deal because higher grade quality humidors don't even have a window through which one could see their content. I compared the results to the analog hygrometer my humidor came with, and the analog one reads about 8-10% less accurate than the digital one. Unsure if the digital one is calibrated correctly, I did the trick of adding salt and water in a back to get a rough baseline of 70% and the digital hygrometer was right on the mark. Good purchase as it also has temperature reading.

The filters are durable and unbleached. The only reason I'm giving the 4 stars is that they do still require a rinsing. Yes, some people will argue that they are tasteless, but when you pour hot water on them the very first time they give off a strong paper smell. This is lessened significantly after a single hot water rinse. I believe it impacts the flavor of the coffee when brewing milder concentrations, which is what I do.  Some people have said you can reuse them, which I have not tried but would like to.  Beware that if you're brewing much more than a liter you will probably need to go up a size. I use these to make 800ml and that's about 2/3 full.  A full one will probably make 1.2 liters but any more and you should get a bigger cone.  Recommend rinsing with boiling water from the kettle just prior to adding the coffee in for maximum effect.

Washing your dishes used to be a non-issue. Even with my "builders" model (read: under $250) dishwasher, pretty much everything got clean. Unfortunately this is no longer the case.
By regulatory action in most states, dish-washing detergents may no longer contain phosphates. With my original dishwasher, the side effects of this change included white, pasty residue over dishware, as well as a coating of ground-up grit evenly distributed and baked-on over everything. These are well-documented problems with phosphate-free detergent.
I'd already tried different premium detergents, and cranked my hot water up to 185F- neither of these issues helped at all. I put in a separate little water heater under the sink so the dishwasher would get its hot water right away- still no change.
The KitchenAid KUDE70 solves these issues very well. Its internal filtering gets rid of all- and I do mean ALL- crud, and I do NOT pre-rinse anything. No more white gunk, either. I turned my hot water temp down to normal, with NO reduction in cleaning quality.
There's a lot more capacity inside, with three spray arms, and an upper flatware tray. There is a "power scrub" mode with special sprayers for cleaning pots. I tested this by burning an egg and leaving the pot set for a day. After washing once- All Clean!
Ah, it's really nice to have clean dishes once again! This unit is definitely worth the cash.
Conditions for this review:
- Length of ownership- about 1 month
- Price Paid: $1239
- Incoming water temp 135F with water tank located near dishwasher
- Detergent used: Cascade powder with Dawn "Shine Shield"
- Rinse Aid: Jet Dry

It does not have the arm at all, so you have to move that over at a minimum. Then I had to move over the small white plastic filler cup, because mine is a "back" fill and not a "side" fill. I also had to move a small plastic bottom bracket.

So keep that in mind. You will have to remove your old one, then lay it side by side with this new on, and start moving parts over from the old to the new. Once you do that, it is then a simple plug in replacement.

This is a heating coil that runs a current through it to generate heat. Many reviews talk about it burning out instantly and frying. That happens when you let the metal coil touch either the mounting board or another part of itself (the lead wires going to the connector). Imagine connecting two ends of a batter together with a wire. Don't do that. As long as you make sure none of it is touching the core or another part of the loop, bend it away so that none of it does, this product is perfect. Also check your positive/negative leads that screw into the side of the housing so that those wires don't cross when you're done. I had no problems flexing the wires a bit to maintain separation.

I've installed this in 2 different models so far, and results were the same: First time it had a slight smell to it but I suspect that was any residual cleaning/production materials burning off and after that it was odorless.

Depending on your dryer, it may be quite an ordeal to get to the housing to replace this. However, despite that, you can do it with just a couple basic tools in a cramped and confined space (I speak from experience) and with just a little effort get a broken dryer unit functioning like new again. I've had one in place for a number of months now and the other almost a year. No problems, no worries, and no regrets so far. I reused the existing thermostats and sensors, however. I have kept those from these kits for backups, but it looked like the factory units were more solid and better constructed.

I would recommend this to anybody that's slightly handy with a couple of tools and needs this repair done. Research your model number and the board you need, they are NOT all universal. Also each dryer opens up slightly differently, although the two I did were very similar internally despite totally different control panels.

P.S. Also take the time to clean all excess lint out of the inside of your dryer when you have it open. Reduce risks of drier lint fires and make sure air vents are not clogged with lint. This product and the dryer in general relies on airflow through the interior of the dryer unit.

I bought this dishwasher magnet so I could stop calling my Mom from across the house to ask whether or not the dishwasher is clean or dirty and so that she could stop bugging me about leaving dishes in the sink after washing them when it takes just two more seconds to actually load it into the dishwasher. She suggested we get one of these magnets, and of course, I suggested we look on Amazon Prime. After looking at all of the hinky dinky little "aesthetic" ones, I just wanted one that was conventional, cute, practical and easy to use and see from a distance. I came across this one for only like, $3.99 that Prime benefits were also included and knew this one was the one. I fell in LOVE with the red/blue combo and the font.

It came in the mail a few days ago, and it's worked perfectly. The magnetization hasn't faltered at all and it sticks to our dishwasher perfectly and holds tight, yet is easy to pry off and flip at the same time. The package comes with a 3M adhesive magnet as well to stick to dishwashers that do not support the magnet itself, but we ended up not having to use it, so we can save it for another time. I think that this is extremely thoughtful of the product makers and sellers to include this, AMAZING marketing and great idea to include this.

One thing I will say is that some of the paint finish on each side arrived chipped. Some of the red on the "DIRTY" side is missing and a chip of the blue on the "CLEAN" side you can see is missing right in the middle under the "E." However, again, it was less than a $5 magnet and nobody should be looking at your dishwasher that closely.

Overall, I highly recommend this magnet. It's one of those "little things" in life, one of those tools, that just makes things so much easier. We've been using it the past few days and already find it easier to just put away dishes or take them out without having to yell from across the house. The magnet is clear to read and sticks great. The packaging comes with an adhesive magnet if it is unable to stick to the dishwasher itself, which is super thoughtful. The magnet is easy to remove, flip and stick back on for ease of use. I highly recommend this if this is a tool that you are looking for.

TL;DR --
Great, powerful magnet that isn't too hard to pull off either. Font is super cute and cool yet practical and simplistic, the red versus blue is easy to see from a far, the magnet itself is easy to pry off and flip for either side, and the packaging includes a 3M adhesive magnet if the magnet does not stick to your dishwasher itself, which was extremely thoughtful. Overall, highly recommend this magnet. It's one of those small tools that makes life easier when life is already difficult; might as well make what you can easier. Love this, great addition to our kitchen and definitely helps out with getting chores like dishes done properly without having to yell from room to room or ask(:

Also go ahead and enable the Tuya skill in Alexa if you are going to use Alexa. For connecting, make sure you phone is on the appropriate 2.4 network, turn on Bluetooth and Location. FYI, when adding any new Wifi device, always be on the correct Network, turn on Bluetooth and Location. It will save you time and make the whole process easier. Then once in your app, install batteries, Click and hold the button on the inside until the light starts blinking. Then click the + to add a new device. The app should find it pretty quick and then it takes less than a minute for it to be added. Then it will display in your Tuya or Smart life app as a new device. I always go in and change the name to where its going to be like Barn sensor and then adjust all my settings. I use F instead of C and I turn off some of the alerts. I leave the alert on for low battery.
If the skill is alredy enabled in Alexa, once the app finds it, Alexa will auto find it immediately. Then you can say things like Alexa, what is the Barn Sensor temperature? And she will tell you. The voice commands work on all Alexa Devices. You can really only find out the Temp or Humidity with Alexa and she will not tell you how much battery is left. I have tried, trust me. Your Settings will impact your battery life. For example, I have the humidity polling every 2 hours, but the temp pulls every 10 minutes. just like any thermometer, it is not an instant read and it takes a while for it to settle once in its location, but then its fairly accurate after the initial 5-10 minutes. I use mine so I can check temperatures in the garage, porch and barn and have alerts set in the garage in case it freezes, so no liquids freeze and burst. I dont really use the app every time I want to check the temp. I just ask Alexa what the temp is on whatever sensor. Battery life for me has been pretty good and have not had to change yet after 2 months. But I dont check every 5 minutes either so it doesnt have to use battery to connect to the wifi. If that makes sense. One improvement would be if you could ask Alexa what percentage of battery is there. For now, I have to go into the app in order to check the battery level which uses up battery lol. Overall, Good device at a good price and works as expected.
